question_text: Two rules run through this grid: one decides the shape and one decides the shading. Which figure belongs in the covered cell?

worked_solution: The shape is set by the column: circles, squares, then triangles. The shading is set by the row: the middle row is filled, the top and bottom rows are empty. The gap is in the third column and middle row, so it is a filled triangle.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text: Three things change at once: the shape, the shading and the size. Which figure comes next?
option_explanations_json: {"A":"The shapes cycle circle, square, triangle, so after the fourth figure (a circle) the shape is a square, not a triangle.","B":"Correct — the shape cycles to square, the shading alternates back to empty, and the size alternates back to small.","C":"Shape and size are right, but the shading alternates empty, filled, empty… so this figure is empty, not filled.","D":"Shape and shading are right, but the size alternates small, large, small… so this figure is small, not large."}
worked_solution: Three rules act together. The shapes cycle circle → square → triangle. The shading alternates empty/filled. The size alternates small/large. The fifth figure is therefore a square (next in the cycle), empty (alternating back), and small (alternating back).

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text: A code shifts every letter forward three places in the alphabet, so A becomes D, B becomes E, and near the end X becomes A, Y becomes B, Z becomes C. Using this code, the word **DOG** is written as **GRJ**. What ordinary word does the coded word **FDW** stand for?
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Shifting CAR forward three gives FDU, not FDW.","B":"BAT shifts to EDW, not FDW.","C":"FAT shifts to IDW, not FDW; here the F was left unchanged instead of being shifted back.","D":"Correct — shifting each code letter back three gives F→C, D→A, W→T, spelling CAT."}
worked_solution: The code shifts each letter forward three places, so to read it we shift each letter back three. F back three is C, D back three is A, and W back three is T. The word is CAT.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Each letter is written as two numbers: its **row** followed by its **column** in the grid below (I and J share a cell).

|   | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | A | B | C | D | E |
| **2** | F | G | H | I/J | K |
| **3** | L | M | N | O | P |
| **4** | Q | R | S | T | U |
| **5** | V | W | X | Y | Z |

What is the code for the word **CAB**?
option_explanations_json: {"A":"This reads column-then-row; the code is row first, then column, so C is 13, not 31.","B":"These are the codes for A, B, C in alphabetical order, but the word is C-A-B, so the order is 13, 11, 12.","C":"Correct — C is row 1 column 3 (13), A is 11, B is 12; in the word’s order that is 13, 11, 12.","D":"The codes are right but B and A are swapped; the word spells C-A-B, giving 13, 11, 12."}
worked_solution: Find each letter and write its row then its column. C is row 1, column 3 (13); A is 11; B is 12. Keeping the order of the word C-A-B gives 13, 11, 12.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text: A square sheet is folded bottom-up so the bottom half lies over the top half. A single hole is punched near the top-left of the folded sheet. When the sheet is unfolded, where are the holes?
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Two holes side by side would happen if the fold were left-to-right; here the fold is bottom-to-top, so the holes are one above the other.","B":"A single hole forgets that the punch goes through both layers, making two holes when unfolded.","C":"Correct — the fold is bottom-to-top, so the crease is a horizontal line across the middle. The hole appears at the same left-right position on both halves, one above the line and one below it.","D":"The holes are mirrored top-to-bottom correctly, but on the wrong side — the punch was near the left, so the holes stay near the left."}
worked_solution: Folding bottom-to-top puts the crease along the horizontal middle line. A punch goes through both layers, so unfolding gives two holes at the same left-right position, one above the middle line and one the same distance below it. The punch was near the left, so both holes are near the left.

worked_solution: The rectangle is 3 wide and 2 tall, so it holds 6 unit squares. The 2×2 square covers 4 of them, leaving a 1×2 strip that the 1×2 piece fills exactly. The other pairs cover the wrong number of squares or leave a gap the pieces cannot fill.

question_text: Each option is a flat arrangement of six squares. Which one will NOT fold up to make a cube?

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text: A solid is built from cubes. Looking straight down (top view) you see a straight row of three squares. Looking from the front you see stacks 2, 1 and 3 cubes tall from left to right. Which solid matches both views?

worked_solution: The top view is a straight row of three, so the three stacks sit in a line. The front view shows the stacks are 2, 1 and 3 cubes tall from left to right. Only the solid that is a straight row with heights 2, 1, 3 matches both views.
